reasoning: The informal claim is the classical Dirichlet integral $\int_0^\infty (\sin x)/x\,dx = \pi/2$. Since $(\sin x)/x$ is not Lebesgue-integrable on $[0,\infty)$, this integral is conventionally interpreted as the improper Riemann integral, namely $\lim_{y\to\infty} \int_0^y (\sin x)/x\,dx$. The Lean statement expresses precisely this: the `intervalIntegral` from 0 to $y$ tends to $\pi/2$ as $y \to \infty$ (`atTop`). The integrand, bounds, and limit value all match the informal assertion, so the formalization is logically equivalent under the standard reading.
